narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a middle-income family background and plans to attend law school full-time, we understand how disappointing it must be to receive this rejection notice for our law program. The admissions model we use predicts your likelihood of passing the bar exam on your first try, based on patterns from thousands of past students' academic records, test scores, and backgrounds. For you specifically, it estimated a 90% chance of failing the bar exam, which is why your application was denied—our goal is to admit students with the strongest predicted odds of bar success to maintain our program's high standards. The biggest factors pulling the prediction toward failure were your Year 3 school grade decile, which placed you in the bottom 10% of your peers, far below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack standing. Your LSAT score was another major influence in the same direction, as it fell well short of the average score seen in students who pass the bar. Your Year 1 grade decile, also in the lowest 10%, added to this concern, ranking as the third strongest signal of potential struggle compared to most applicants. Less influential but still noted were your undergraduate GPA and family income bracket, which didn't provide much counterbalance here. Interestingly, your choice to study full-time nudged things slightly toward a passing prediction, aligning with the vast majority of successful students. Overall, your profile stands out as weaker than typical admitted students, especially in early grades and standardized testing, where averages hover much higher. This isn't a judgment on your potential or character—many students overcome similar starts with targeted effort. We recommend retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your undergraduate record if possible through post-bac courses, and seeking academic advising to improve your class rankings before reapplying. You're capable of turning this around, and we're rooting for your success in law. Keep pursuing your goals—we'd welcome a stronger application from you in the future.
